title: Price Versus Value
html:
Two different numbers

The single most important idea in valuation is that price and value are not the same thing. Price is what the market quotes you right now — the last trade, blinking on a screen, set by the marginal buyer and seller. Value is what the underlying business is actually worth: the cash it will generate for its owners over its life, discounted back to today. Price is a fact. Value is an estimate. The gap between them is where opportunity lives.

Benjamin Graham put it as a parable: imagine a manic business partner named Mr. Market who shows up every day and offers to buy your shares or sell you his, at wildly swinging prices driven by his mood. Some days he is euphoric and quotes you absurdly high; some days he is despondent and quotes you absurdly low. You are never obligated to transact. His quote tells you his mood — not the worth of your business. Your job is to know the value independently, so that you can exploit his moods rather than be ruled by them.

A concrete gap

Suppose ACME Industries trades at 142.50 per share. You build a discounted-cash-flow model (we will do this step by step later in the part) and conclude the business is worth about $160 per share. The market price and your value estimate disagree by $17.50. Expressed as a cushion below value, that is your margin of safety.

Price vs value
Market price = 142.50 Estimated intrinsic value = 160.00 Discount to value = 160.00 − 142.50 = 17.50 Margin of safety = 17.50 ÷ 160.00 = 10.9% ≈ 11%

Note the denominator: margin of safety is measured against value, not price, because value is the anchor you trust. An 11% cushion means the business could be worth 11% less than your estimate and you would still merely break even at today's price.

Definition.
Margin of safety = (intrinsic value − price) ÷ intrinsic value. It is the discount you demand to protect against the error built into every estimate.

Why the gap exists

If markets were perfectly efficient, price would always equal value and this entire part would be pointless. In practice gaps open because of fear and greed, forced selling, index flows, short-term earnings noise, neglect of small or boring companies, and plain disagreement about the future. The gap is not a guarantee — it is a probabilistic edge. You will be wrong about value sometimes; the margin of safety is what keeps those errors survivable.

In the chart, the upper horizontal line marks the estimated intrinsic value at $160 and the lower line marks the current price at 142.50. The shaded zone between them is the margin of safety. Crucially, the intrinsic-value line is roughly flat while price swings around it — that is the whole point. Value changes slowly as the business evolves; price changes by the second as sentiment churns. A value investor watches price oscillate around a more stable value and acts only when the gap is wide enough.

Value is a range, not a point

A common beginner error is to treat $160 as a precise truth. It is not. Change one growth assumption and it might be $150 or $172. Mature valuation thinking produces a range — say $145 to $175 — and then asks whether the current price sits comfortably below the low end. At 142.50, ACME is below even the conservative $145, which is a far stronger signal than being below a single midpoint estimate. We will quantify these ranges with sensitivity analysis in Lesson 3.8.

Insight.
You do not need to know exactly what a business is worth to act intelligently. You only need to know whether it is worth meaningfully more — or less — than its price.
Pitfall.
A cheap-looking price is not a margin of safety if your value estimate is wrong. "Value traps" are stocks that look cheap against a deteriorating business whose true value keeps falling toward the price. Always test whether value is stable before trusting the gap.
▸Try It

Build the price-versus-value habit on a real holding or watchlist name.

  1. Write down the current market price of one stock you follow.
  2. Find any third-party estimate of its "fair value" or analyst target and treat it as a rough value anchor.
  3. Compute the gap and the margin of safety as a percentage of value: (value − price) ÷ value.
  4. Decide: is the gap large enough that you would still feel safe if your value estimate were 15% too high?

title: Relative Valuation: Multiples and When to Use Them
html:
The shortcut: pricing by ratio

A multiple values a company by expressing its price as a ratio to some fundamental number — earnings, cash flow, or sales. Instead of forecasting decades of cash, you ask: what is the market paying per dollar of this thing, and is that reasonable versus peers and history? Multiples are fast, intuitive, and grounded in observable market prices. Their weakness is that they smuggle in assumptions you never state explicitly. Mastering them means knowing which multiple fits which company.

P/E — price to earnings

The most famous multiple. P/E = Price ÷ EPS. It tells you how many dollars investors pay for one dollar of annual earnings. A P/E of 17 means $17 of price per $1 of yearly profit. Use forward P/E (next year's expected EPS) for decisions and trailing P/E (last year's actual) for sanity-checking.

P/E example
Price = 142.50 Forward EPS = 8.65 P/E = 142.50 ÷ 8.65 = 16.5×

P/E works well for profitable, stable companies with comparable capital structures. It breaks down when earnings are negative (the ratio becomes meaningless), volatile, or distorted by one-time charges. It also ignores debt: two firms with identical earnings but very different leverage can show the same P/E while being radically different in risk.

EV/EBITDA — enterprise value to operating cash earnings

This multiple fixes P/E's blind spot on capital structure. Enterprise value is what it would cost to buy the whole business free of its financing: EV = market cap + total debt − cash. EBITDA is earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization — a rough proxy for operating cash generation before financing and accounting choices. Because EV includes debt and EBITDA is pre-interest, the ratio is capital-structure neutral: you can compare a debt-heavy firm to a debt-free one on equal footing.

EV/EBITDA for a leveraged industrial
Market cap = 4,200 + Total debt = 2,600 − Cash = 300 Enterprise value = 4,200 + 2,600 − 300 = 6,500 EBITDA = 810 EV/EBITDA = 6,500 ÷ 810 = 8.0×

EV/EBITDA is the default for capital-intensive, leveraged businesses — industrials, telecom, cable, utilities — and for comparing across firms with different debt loads. Its caveat: EBITDA ignores the real cash cost of replacing worn-out equipment (capex). For a business that must constantly reinvest, EBITDA flatters the picture; pair it with EV/EBIT or free-cash-flow yield.

P/S — price to sales

P/S = Market cap ÷ revenue. Sales are the one line that is almost always positive, so P/S is the tool of last resort when there are no earnings to divide by. That is exactly the situation of a fast-growing software company plowing every dollar into growth and reporting accounting losses.

P/S for an unprofitable software firm
Market cap = 9,000 Revenue = 1,000 Net income = −120  (loss → P/E undefined) P/S = 9,000 ÷ 1,000 = 9.0×

P/S only makes sense if you also have a view on future margins. A 9× sales multiple is cheap if the firm will one day earn 30% net margins, and ruinous if it tops out at 5%. Always translate P/S into an implied future P/E before trusting it.

Rule of thumb.
Profitable & comparable leverage → P/E. Leveraged or capital-intensive → EV/EBITDA. Unprofitable but growing → P/S (with a margin thesis attached).

The reasoning, side by side

SituationBest multipleWhy
Mature consumer brand, modest debtP/EEarnings are real, stable, comparable
Leveraged industrial / utilityEV/EBITDANeutralizes different debt loads
High-growth, loss-making SaaSP/SNo earnings; revenue is the only stable anchor
Bank or insurerP/B, P/EBalance sheet drives value; EBITDA meaningless
Cyclical at peak earningsEV/EBITDA on normalized figuresTrailing P/E looks deceptively low at the top
Pitfall.
A low multiple is not automatically cheap and a high multiple is not automatically expensive. A 9× P/E can be dear for a company in structural decline; a 30× P/E can be cheap for one compounding earnings at 25% a year. Multiples are relative — they only have meaning against growth, risk, and peers.
▸Try It

Pick the right multiple for three different businesses.

  1. Choose a profitable consumer company, a debt-heavy industrial, and an unprofitable growth firm.
  2. For each, compute the multiple from the rule of thumb (P/E, EV/EBITDA, P/S respectively).
  3. Write one sentence justifying why that multiple, and not the others, fits the company.
  4. For the P/S name, estimate a plausible mature net margin and convert the P/S into an implied forward P/E.

title: Building a Comparables Table
html:
Valuation by company you keep

A comparables analysis ("comps") values a company by benchmarking its multiples against a set of similar businesses. The logic is simple: if the market pays roughly 17× earnings for companies like this one, and our company earns $8.65 per share, then a fair price is about 17 × $8.65. Comps anchor your estimate in what investors are actually paying today for comparable risk and growth, which is a useful cross-check against the more abstract DCF.

Step 1 — choose a clean peer set

The whole method lives or dies on peer selection. Good peers share the company's industry, business model, growth rate, margins, and capital intensity. Bad peers share only a sector label. For Sample Co — a mid-cap branded products company — we pick five peers of similar size and growth, deliberately excluding a turnaround case and a much faster grower that would distort the average.

Peer set screen
Include → similar revenue, 4-8% growth, 12-16% margins Exclude → distressed turnaround (depressed EPS distorts P/E) Exclude → hyper-grower at 30% (commands a premium multiple)

Step 2 — build the table

Pull each peer's forward P/E. Real comps tables carry several multiples (EV/EBITDA, P/S, EV/Sales) side by side, but we will follow P/E here for clarity.

PeerPriceFwd EPSFwd P/E
BravoCorp96.006.0016.0×
CedarWorks132.007.5017.6×
DeltaGoods88.005.2016.9×
EchoBrands210.0011.7017.9×
FoxtrotInc54.003.3016.4×

Step 3 — pick a central tendency

Average the peer multiples — but check the median too, because a single outlier can drag the mean. Here mean and median nearly agree, which gives confidence the set is clean.

Central P/E
Sum = 16.0 + 17.6 + 16.9 + 17.9 + 16.4 = 84.8 Mean P/E = 84.8 ÷ 5 = 16.96× ≈ 17× Median P/E = 16.9×  (agrees → set is clean)

Step 4 — apply to Sample Co

Multiply the peer-average multiple by Sample Co's own forward EPS to back out an implied fair value.

Implied fair value
Peer-average forward P/E = 17× Sample Co forward EPS = 8.65 Fair value = 17 × 8.65 = 147.05 ≈ $147

So the comps approach says Sample Co is worth about $147 per share. Against a market price of 142.50, the stock sits slightly below peer-implied fair value — a modest discount, consistent with (and independent of) the DCF we will build next.

Adjust for differences

Sample Co is rarely the exact average of its peers. If it grows faster or earns higher margins than the group, it deserves a premium multiple; if it is more leveraged or slower-growing, a discount. Suppose Sample Co's margins run two points above the peer mean — you might justify nudging the applied multiple to 17.5×, lifting fair value to about $151. Document every such adjustment; an undefended premium is just wishful thinking.

Insight.
Comps tell you what the market currently pays for similar businesses. They are powerful when the whole peer group is fairly priced — and dangerous when the entire sector is in a bubble or a panic, because comps inherit the crowd's error.
Pitfall.
Garbage peers produce garbage values. A loss-making peer (negative EPS) silently corrupts an average P/E; a peer with a different fiscal year or a one-time gain distorts the multiple. Inspect every row before trusting the mean.
Formula.
Implied value per share = peer-average multiple × company's own per-share metric (EPS for P/E, EBITDA-per-share for EV/EBITDA, etc.).
▸Try It

Build a five-peer comps table and value a target company.

  1. Pick a target company and find five genuine peers (similar size, growth, margins).
  2. For each peer, record price and forward EPS, then compute forward P/E.
  3. Calculate both the mean and median P/E; if they diverge sharply, hunt for the outlier and decide whether to drop it.
  4. Apply the central multiple to your target's forward EPS to get an implied fair value, then adjust up or down for any growth or margin differences — and justify the adjustment in one sentence.

title: DCF Step 1: Projecting Free Cash Flow
html:
What a DCF actually claims

A discounted cash flow model rests on one idea: a business is worth the sum of all the cash it will hand to its owners in the future, with each future dollar shrunk to its value today. There are three steps — (1) project the cash, (2) choose a discount rate, (3) add a terminal value and total it up. This lesson is step one: forecasting free cash flow.

Free cash flow defined

Free cash flow (FCF) is the cash a business generates after paying to keep itself running and growing — the cash genuinely available to all investors. The standard build:

Formula.
FCF = operating cash flow − capital expenditures. Or from earnings: FCF = EBIT × (1 − tax) + depreciation & amortization − capex − change in working capital.

We use unlevered free cash flow (FCF to the whole firm, before interest) so it pairs with WACC and enterprise value in the next lessons. The discipline is to forecast it from drivers — revenue growth and margins — not to pull a number from the air.

The decay principle

The cardinal rule of forecasting: growth fades. No company compounds at a high rate forever; competition, market saturation, and the law of large numbers pull every growth rate toward the economy's. So a credible forecast decays the growth rate year by year toward a sustainable long-run figure. We start Sample Co at $600M of FCF and fade growth from 10% down to 4% over five explicit years.

Five-year FCF projection (USD millions)
Base FCF (year 0) = 600 Year 1: 600 × 1.10 = 660.0  (+10%) Year 2: 660.0 × 1.08 = 712.8  (+8%) Year 3: 712.8 × 1.06 = 755.6  (+6%) Year 4: 755.6 × 1.05 = 793.4  (+5%) Year 5: 793.4 × 1.04 = 825.1  (+4%)

The decaying-growth path lands Sample Co near $825M of FCF in year five — in the neighborhood of the roughly $880M you would reach with slightly richer assumptions, and a far more honest figure than naively extending 10% growth (which would imply $966M and overstate value). The exact number matters less than the shape: rising cash, decelerating growth.

Forecasting from drivers, not from FCF directly

In a real model you do not grow FCF as one lump; you build it from revenue and margin so the assumptions are inspectable. Watch how the same year-1 figure emerges from drivers:

Year 1 FCF from drivers (USD millions)
Revenue = 5,000 × 1.07 = 5,350  (7% sales growth) EBIT margin = 18% → EBIT = 963 − tax @ 21% → NOPAT = 963 × 0.79 = 760.8 + D&A = +250 − capex = −300 − Δ working capital = −51 Unlevered FCF = 760.8 + 250 − 300 − 51 ≈ 660

This is the discipline that separates a model from a guess: every FCF number traces back to a revenue assumption, a margin, a tax rate, and reinvestment. If a reviewer disputes your value, they can argue with a specific lever — "I think the EBIT margin holds at 18%, not your assumed expansion" — rather than waving at a black box.

Insight.
The early forecast years drive a surprisingly small share of total value — often well under half. Most DCF value lives in the terminal value (Lesson 3.6). So get the long-run growth and margin trend right; do not obsess over year-2 precision.
Pitfall.
The "hockey stick" — flat history followed by a forecast that suddenly accelerates — is the most common modeling sin. If your projected growth exceeds the company's recent reality, you must name the specific catalyst (new product, capacity, pricing power) that justifies it, or decay the rate down instead.
▸Try It

Project five years of free cash flow for a company you follow.

  1. Find its most recent annual free cash flow (operating cash flow minus capex).
  2. Choose a year-1 growth rate from its recent trend, then decay it down toward 3-4% by year five.
  3. Compute FCF for each of the five years by compounding the decaying rates.
  4. Cross-check year 1 by rebuilding it from revenue growth and an EBIT margin — do the two approaches roughly agree?

title: DCF Step 2: The Discount Rate and WACC
html:
Why future cash is worth less

A dollar in five years is worth less than a dollar today — you could have invested today's dollar, and the future is uncertain. The discount rate converts future cash into present value and encodes the riskiness of those cash flows: riskier cash is discounted harder. For unlevered free cash flow that belongs to the whole firm, the correct rate is the weighted average cost of capital (WACC) — the blended return demanded by all the company's investors, both equity and debt.

Formula.
WACC = (E÷V) × re + (D÷V) × rd × (1 − tax), where E is equity value, D is debt, V = E + D, re is cost of equity, rd is cost of debt.

The cost of equity via CAPM

Equity holders are last in line and bear the most risk, so they demand the highest return. The Capital Asset Pricing Model estimates that required return:

Formula.
re = rf + β × (rm − rf). Risk-free rate plus beta times the equity risk premium.

The risk-free rate (rf) is typically the 10-year government bond yield. Beta (β) measures how much the stock moves relative to the market: β = 1 moves with the market, β > 1 is more volatile, β < 1 less so. The equity risk premium (rm − rf) is the extra return stocks have historically paid over bonds — commonly taken near 4–5%.

Cost of equity (CAPM)
Risk-free rate rf = 4.0% Beta β = 1.25 Equity risk premium = 4.4% re = 4.0% + 1.25 × 4.4% = 4.0% + 5.5% = 9.5%

The cost of debt

Debt is cheaper than equity for two reasons: lenders are paid before shareholders, and interest is tax-deductible. Take the company's effective borrowing rate and multiply by (1 − tax) to get the after-tax cost. Here a pre-tax rate near 5.06% at a 21% tax rate gives roughly 4%.

After-tax cost of debt
Pre-tax cost of debt = 5.06% Tax rate = 21% rd after tax = 5.06% × (1 − 0.21) = 4.0%

Blending into WACC

Now weight the two by their share of total capital. Sample Co funds itself roughly 70% equity, 30% debt (using market values, not book values — this matters).

WACC
Equity weight E÷V = 70%   at re = 9.5% Debt weight D÷V = 30%   at rd = 4.0% WACC = 0.70 × 9.5% + 0.30 × 4.0% = 6.65% + 1.20% = 7.85% ≈ 7.9%

Sample Co's WACC is about 7.9%. This is the rate we will use to discount every projected free cash flow in the next lesson. Read it intuitively: the company must earn at least 7.9% on its capital to satisfy its investors; cash flows are worth more the lower this hurdle, and less the higher.

Insight.
WACC is the single most powerful lever in a DCF. Moving it from 7.9% to 8.9% can knock 15-20% off the valuation, because it discounts every future year and compounds in the terminal value. Treat your discount rate with the same care as your growth rate — we will stress both in Lesson 3.8.
Pitfall.
Using book weights instead of market weights, or forgetting the (1 − tax) on debt, are the two classic WACC errors. Also resist false precision: WACC is an estimate good to maybe a quarter-point, so 7.9% really means "call it 8%."
▸Try It

Estimate the WACC for a company you are analyzing.

  1. Look up the current 10-year government bond yield (risk-free rate) and the company's beta.
  2. Compute cost of equity with CAPM using a 4.5% equity risk premium: rf + β × 4.5%.
  3. Find the company's borrowing rate and tax rate, then compute after-tax cost of debt.
  4. Estimate market-value equity and debt weights and blend the two costs into WACC. Then recompute it with the equity weight 10 points higher to see how sensitive it is.

title: DCF Step 3: Terminal Value and Intrinsic Value
html:
Value beyond the forecast

We projected five years of free cash flow (Lesson 3.4) and a 7.9% WACC (Lesson 3.5). But a healthy business does not stop generating cash in year six — it keeps going indefinitely. The terminal value captures all the cash flows after the explicit forecast, collapsed into a single number at the end of year five. For most companies, terminal value is the largest component of the valuation, so getting it right is decisive.

The Gordon growth method

The cleanest approach assumes that after year five, FCF grows forever at a modest, sustainable rate g — never above the long-run growth of the economy (roughly 2–3%), or you would be implying the company eventually swallows the world.

Formula.
Terminal value (at end of year 5) = FCF5 × (1 + g) ÷ (WACC − g). Then discount that lump back to today like any other future cash flow.
Terminal value (USD millions)
Year-5 FCF = 825.1 Terminal growth g = 2.5% WACC = 7.9% TV5 = 825.1 × 1.025 ÷ (0.079 − 0.025) = 845.7 ÷ 0.054 = 15,661

Discounting every cash flow to today

Each future amount is divided by (1 + WACC)t, where t is the number of years away. The terminal value, sitting at the end of year 5, is discounted by the same year-5 factor.

Present value of cash flows (USD millions, WACC 7.9%)
Yr1 660.0 ÷ 1.0791 = 611.7 Yr2 712.8 ÷ 1.0792 = 612.3 Yr3 755.6 ÷ 1.0793 = 601.5 Yr4 793.4 ÷ 1.0794 = 585.3 Yr5 825.1 ÷ 1.0795 = 564.2 TV 15,661 ÷ 1.0795 = 10,709 Enterprise value = sum = 13,684

Notice that the discounted terminal value (10,709) is about 78% of the total enterprise value — a vivid illustration of why long-run assumptions dominate. The five explicit years together contribute under a quarter of the value.

From enterprise value to value per share

Enterprise value is the worth of the whole business to all capital providers. To reach value per share, subtract net debt (debt minus cash) to get equity value, then divide by shares outstanding.

Per-share intrinsic value
Enterprise value = 13,684 − Net debt (debt 2,000 − cash 300) = −1,700 Equity value = 13,684 − 1,700 = 11,984 ÷ Shares outstanding = 75.0M Intrinsic value per share = 11,984 ÷ 75 = $159.8 ≈ $160

The DCF concludes Sample Co is worth about $160 per share — the very figure introduced back in Lesson 3.1. Against a market price of 142.50, that is an 11% discount to intrinsic value. Reassuringly, it sits in the same neighborhood as the $147 comps estimate, with the DCF a touch higher because it gives full credit to the company's long-run cash generation.

Insight.
Always sanity-check terminal value with an exit multiple: divide it by year-5 FCF (15,661 ÷ 825 ≈ 19×). If that implied multiple is wildly out of line with what such businesses actually trade for, your growth or discount assumption is off.
Pitfall.
The terminal-value formula is explosive near WACC = g. Setting g too close to WACC (say 6% growth against 7.9% WACC) sends the denominator toward zero and the value to absurdity. Keep g safely below WACC and at or under long-run GDP growth.
▸Try It

Complete a DCF you started in Lesson 3.4.

  1. Take your year-5 FCF and compute terminal value with g = 2.5% and your WACC.
  2. Discount each of the five FCFs and the terminal value back to today using (1 + WACC) raised to the year.
  3. Sum them for enterprise value, subtract net debt for equity value, divide by shares for per-share value.
  4. Check the implied exit multiple (terminal value ÷ year-5 FCF) for sanity, and compare your per-share value to the current price.

title: The Dividend Discount Model
html:
Valuing the cash you actually receive

For a shareholder in a stable company, the cash that physically reaches you is the dividend. The dividend discount model (DDM) takes this literally: a share is worth the present value of all future dividends. When dividends grow at a steady rate forever, the infinite sum collapses into one elegant equation — the Gordon growth model, the same mathematics behind a DCF terminal value, applied to dividends instead of free cash flow.

Formula.
Value per share = D1 ÷ (r − g), where D1 is next year's dividend, r is the required return (cost of equity), and g is the perpetual dividend growth rate. Note D1 = D0 × (1 + g).

A worked example

Take a mature utility-like company paying a current dividend of $2.40, which has reliably grown about 4% a year. Investors require an 8% return on the equity. First grow the dividend one year forward, then apply the formula.

Gordon growth valuation
Current dividend D0 = 2.40 Growth g = 4% Required return r = 8% D1 = 2.40 × 1.04 = 2.496 Value = 2.496 ÷ (0.08 − 0.04) = 2.496 ÷ 0.04 = $62.40

The model values the share at $62.40. The intuition: a $2.50 dividend that grows 4% forever, when you demand 8%, behaves like a stream worth 4% — the spread between return and growth — per year, so you pay roughly 25 times the forward dividend (1 ÷ 0.04). If the same stock traded at $55, the model would flag it as undervalued; at $75, overvalued.

Why the spread (r − g) is everything

The entire valuation hinges on the gap between the required return and the growth rate. The denominator is small, so tiny changes swing the value enormously — exactly the sensitivity we saw in the DCF terminal value.

Sensitivity of value to (r − g)
g = 3%, r = 8% → 2.472 ÷ 0.05 = $49.44 g = 4%, r = 8% → 2.496 ÷ 0.04 = $62.40 g = 5%, r = 8% → 2.520 ÷ 0.03 = $84.00 g = 6%, r = 8% → 2.544 ÷ 0.02 = $127.20

A single percentage point of assumed growth moves the value from $62 to $84 — and as g approaches r the value runs away to nonsense. This fragility is the model's defining weakness.

When the DDM fits — and when it fails

The DDM is excellent for mature, stable, reliably dividend-paying companies: regulated utilities, consumer staples, established banks — firms with long, smooth dividend histories and growth comfortably below the required return. It fails badly for:

  • Non-dividend payers — growth and tech firms returning cash via buybacks or reinvestment have D0 = 0, and the model returns zero.
  • High or erratic growth — when g exceeds or nears r, the formula breaks; you need a multi-stage model (high growth fading to a stable tail).
  • Companies whose payout doesn't track earnings — if dividends are propped up beyond what cash flow supports, the model values an unsustainable promise.
Insight.
A buyback is an economic cousin of a dividend — both return cash to owners. For firms that favor repurchases, value the total shareholder yield (dividends + net buybacks) rather than dividends alone, or you will badly understate the stock.
Pitfall.
Forcing the DDM onto a fast grower by plugging in a high g produces a wildly inflated value precisely because (r − g) collapses. If g is anywhere near r, abandon the single-stage model and use a two-stage approach or a full DCF instead.
▸Try It

Value a dividend payer with the Gordon growth model.

  1. Pick a mature, consistent dividend-paying company and note its current annual dividend per share.
  2. Estimate a sustainable long-run growth rate g from its dividend history (keep it below the required return).
  3. Use a required return r near its cost of equity, then compute value = D0 × (1 + g) ÷ (r − g).
  4. Rebuild the value with g one point higher and one point lower to feel how sensitive it is, then compare your range to the market price.

title: Sensitivity, Margin of Safety and a Full Valuation
html:
One number is a lie; a range is the truth

Our DCF produced an intrinsic value of about $160 for Sample Co. But that single figure rests on two estimates we can never know exactly: the discount rate (WACC) and the terminal growth rate (g). Mature valuation does not report one number — it maps how the value moves as those assumptions flex. That map is the sensitivity analysis, and it transforms a brittle point estimate into an honest range.

The sensitivity grid

We rebuild the DCF across a grid of WACC and g combinations. Lower WACC and higher g push value up; higher WACC and lower g push it down. Each cell is the full per-share intrinsic value recomputed end to end.

Intrinsic value per share — WACC (rows) × terminal g (cols)
g=2.0% g=2.5% g=3.0% WACC 7.0% 178 189 198 WACC 7.9% 152 160 168 WACC 9.0% 128 133 139

The grid spans roughly $128 to $198 per share. Read it carefully. The base case (7.9% WACC, 2.5% g) sits at $160 in the center. The bearish corner — a demanding 9% discount rate and feeble 2% growth — still produces $128. The bullish corner reaches $198. This is the actual output of a valuation: not "$160," but "$128 on pessimistic assumptions, $160 base, $198 optimistic."

Reading the grid against the price

Now overlay the market price of 142.50. The crucial observation: 142.50 falls below the base case ($160) and, more importantly, the stock is worth more than its price across most of the grid. Even in the fairly conservative 9% WACC / 3% g cell, value is $139 — still under price by a hair, which marks the rough boundary of the bear case.

The chart frames the decision visually: price (lower line) trades beneath the base-case value (upper line), and the shaded band shows the full range of plausible value. The buy thesis is that price sits in the lower third of the band — you are paying 142.50 for something worth $160 in the base case and at least $128 even if you are meaningfully wrong.

Quantifying the margin of safety

Margin of safety at 142.50
vs base value 160 → (160 − 142.5) ÷ 160 = 10.9% vs bear value 128 → (128 − 142.5) ÷ 128 = −11.3% vs comps 147 → (147 − 142.5) ÷ 147 = 3.1%

The honest reckoning: an 11% margin of safety to the base case, a comfortable upside to the bull case, but the bear case ($128) sits below today's price. So this is not a fat-pitch, deep-value bargain — it is a moderately undervalued, reasonable-quality business. Whether 11% is enough depends on your conviction in the assumptions and the quality of the company: for a durable franchise an 11% discount may suffice; for a riskier business you would demand 30–40%.

Tying every number back to the business

Reconcile the threads. The comps said ~$147 (what the market pays for peers today). The DCF said ~$160 (the discounted cash the business itself will generate). The sensitivity grid said $128–$198 (the range across honest assumption error). All three are independent roads to roughly the same neighborhood — the convergence is what gives confidence. The quoted price of 142.50 is simply Mr. Market's mood today; our work says the business is worth somewhat more.

The full process.
(1) Forecast FCF from drivers → (2) discount at WACC → (3) add terminal value for intrinsic value → (4) cross-check with comps → (5) flex assumptions into a range → (6) buy only with a margin of safety appropriate to the risk.
Insight.
Convergence of independent methods is the strongest signal in valuation. When a DCF, a comps table, and a dividend model all point to a similar value, your estimate is robust. When they diverge sharply, that disagreement is itself information — find out which assumption drives the gap.
Pitfall.
Sensitivity analysis can become "assumption laundering": quietly choosing the rosy corner of the grid to justify a purchase you already wanted. Anchor on the base case and the bear case, not the bull corner, and let the price-versus-value gap — not your hopes — drive the decision.
▸Try It

Stress-test a full valuation and frame a buy decision.

  1. Take your completed DCF and recompute per-share value across three WACC levels (base, +1%, −1%) and three g levels (2%, 2.5%, 3%) — a 3×3 grid.
  2. Note the lowest (bear) and highest (bull) values and the base-case center.
  3. Compute the margin of safety of the current price against both the base case and the bear case.
  4. Decide: is the discount large enough for the quality and riskiness of this business? Cross-check against a comps value before concluding.
